patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Open with: “I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time.” Early in the visit, volunteer: constant urination including overnight, drinking water nonstop due to dry mouth, waking 3–5 times nightly to pee, and daytime exhaustion from poor sleep.
If asked about details, add: urine looks almost clear, started suddenly over the last couple weeks, you carry a big water bottle, occasional headaches, lightheaded if you can’t drink right away, and a few pounds of unintentional weight loss because you’re too busy to eat.
Do NOT volunteer unless specifically asked: craving ice/cold water, panic without water, dry skin/lips, constipation, shakiness/foggy feeling when deprived, racing heart when very thirsty, avoiding drives/meetings due to bathroom fear.
Be reluctant/embarrassed unless asked directly: a couple accidents, wearing a pad at night, lower sex drive.
Express misconceptions: worry it’s diabetes; wonder about coffee/energy drinks, “detox” water intake, or UTI (no burning).
If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and admit you’re scared and it’s affecting work. Mention cost concerns if treatment/testing comes up.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for headache
comorbidities: History of mild traumatic brain injury (concussion) 6 months ago
confounders: High daily caffeine intake (energy drinks), contributing to diuresis
Sertraline-associated xerostomia and increased thirst sensation
Recent concussion history raises concern for post-traumatic pituitary dysfunction versus primary polydipsia
Intermittent NSAID use can reduce urine output and partially mask severity of diabetes insipidus

required_evidence: Polyuria with elevated 24-hour urine volume (typically >3 L/day in adults) and dilute urine (low urine osmolality/low specific gravity)
Serum osmolality high or high-normal with serum sodium high or high-normal in the absence of appropriate urine concentration
Water deprivation test shows failure to appropriately concentrate urine (urine osmolality remains low with dehydration)
Desmopressin challenge produces a significant rise in urine osmolality (classically >50% increase), consistent with central DI rather than nephrogenic DI

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium: Normal-high to elevated (often >145 mmol/L if water intake is insufficient)
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: High or high-normal (often >295 mOsm/kg) with inappropriately dilute urine
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality (baseline): Low (often <300 mOsm/kg, frequently <200 mOsm/kg) despite polyuria
expected_lab_results.Water deprivation test - urine osmolalit: Remains low with minimal rise (fails to concentrate appropriately; often stays <300 mOsm/kg)
expected_lab_results.Desmopressin challenge - urine osmolalit: Marked increase (often rises to >300–600 mOsm/kg depending on partial vs complete central DI)

medications: Desmopressin (DDAVP) 0.05 mg PO twice daily, titrate to control polyuria/polydipsia while avoiding hyponatremia
non_pharmacological: Ensure free access to water; drink to thirst (do not force fluids once on DDAVP)
Monitor for symptoms of hyponatremia (headache, nausea, confusion) and hypernatremia/dehydration (thirst, dizziness, weakness)
Daily weights initially if high risk; track urine volume and nocturia to guide titration
Review potential underlying causes (e.g., pituitary/hypothalamic disease); arrange endocrine evaluation and consider pituitary MRI if not previously done
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck serum sodium and clinical response within 3–7 days after starting or changing DDAVP dose; then again at 2–4 weeks and periodically thereafter

must_include: Condition is due to insufficient antidiuretic hormone (vasopressin) production/release from the brain, causing inability of kidneys to concentrate urine
How the water deprivation and desmopressin tests support central DI (urine stays dilute with dehydration but concentrates after DDAVP)
Differentiate from diabetes mellitus (not related to blood sugar)
Desmopressin replaces the missing hormone and reduces urine volume and thirst
Dose titration goals: control symptoms while preventing low sodium
Key adverse effect: hyponatremia/water intoxication—avoid excessive fluid intake once controlled
When to seek urgent care: confusion, severe headache, seizures (possible hyponatremia); severe dehydration, fainting (possible hypernatremia)
Discuss special situations: vomiting/diarrhea, inability to access water, missed doses, perioperative/illness planning
Confirm penicillin allergy is noted; clarify that DDAVP is not related to penicillin
Drink to thirst; do not overhydrate on DDAVP
Keep a log of urine output/nocturia and daily weight early in treatment
Adherence and timing (often bedtime dose helps nocturia); avoid doubling doses if a dose is missed without clinician guidance

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms, timeline, triggers, severity, fluid intake, nocturia, and red flags; clarify prior testing (water deprivation/desmopressin) and differentiate from diabetes mellitus.

Obtain/confirm objective data supporting central DI and assess safety for treatment: serum sodium, serum osmolality, urine osmolality/specific gravity, glucose; consider renal function and pregnancy status when relevant.
Review labs and any water deprivation/desmopressin test results to confirm diagnosis and establish baseline sodium/osmolality prior to desmopressin.
Formally diagnose central diabetes insipidus based on history and test pattern (dilute urine despite dehydration with subsequent concentration after desmopressin).
Required safety check before prescribing; confirm and document allergies (including penicillin) and clarify non-cross-reactivity with desmopressin.
Screen for clinically relevant interactions that increase hyponatremia risk (e.g., SSRIs, carbamazepine, thiazides, NSAIDs) and adjust plan/monitoring.
Initiate desmopressin with a conservative starting regimen and explicit titration/monitoring plan to control symptoms while preventing hyponatremia.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and testing, explain treatment and hyponatremia prevention, address urgent symptoms and special situations, and provide lifestyle/adherence guidance.
Arrange timely follow-up and lab monitoring (especially serum sodium) after starting or changing desmopressin; provide contingency plan for illness or missed doses.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 28-year-old man. Be vague and a bit evasive; give short answers unless asked directly. Start by saying: “My legs are getting stiffer and I keep tripping more than I used to.” Volunteer only: legs feel tight/stiff (worse when walking fast), more stumbling/falls over months, tired after short walks, feet sometimes drag. If asked about other symptoms, mention: past shaking spells and you take seizure medicine, legs jerk at night, constipation, urgent urination with occasional accidents, speech gets stuck when nervous/rushed, you’ve always needed extra help with school/paperwork. correct_diagnosis.primary: Autosomal dominant intellectual disability (ADID), likely syndromic—requires genetic confirmation
acceptable_alternatives: Autosomal dominant syndromic neurodevelopmental disorder (e.g., ADID due to pathogenic variant in a known AD gene)
Intellectual developmental disorder with autosomal dominant inheritance (non-syndromic)
required_evidence: History of lifelong global developmental delay/intellectual disability with onset in childhood
Adaptive functioning impairment across conceptual/social/practical domains
Family history consistent with autosomal dominant inheritance (affected first-degree relatives across generations) OR confirmed pathogenic variant with AD inheritance
Exclusion of acquired causes (no history suggesting hypoxic injury, CNS infection, significant head trauma, severe perinatal insult)
Consideration/documentation of associated neurobehavioral features (e.g., autism traits, ADHD, seizures) and dysmorphic/syndromic features if present

expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (WBC): Typically normal (no specific diagnostic abnormality expected)

non_pharmacological: Confirm etiology and inheritance: refer to Medical Genetics for targeted gene panel/whole exome (or review prior genetic testing) and genetic counseling
Neurodevelopmental assessment: formal cognitive/adaptive testing; screen for comorbid ASD/ADHD/anxiety/depression and seizures; refer to Neurology if seizure history or concerning episodes

Therapies and supports: individualized education plan (IEP) / supported employment; speech-language therapy, occupational therapy, physical therapy as indicated; behavioral therapy (e.g., ABA/skills-based interventions) when appropriate
Safety and function: assess decision-making capacity/guardianship needs; evaluate home/school/work safety; consider social work/case management for community resources
Health maintenance: vision/hearing screening; sleep assessment; nutrition/exercise counseling; ensure routine vaccinations and preventive care
Family planning: discuss recurrence risk, options for prenatal/preimplantation genetic testing if a pathogenic variant is identified
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-8 weeks to review genetic/testing results and coordinate services; sooner (1-2 weeks) if significant behavioral/safety concerns or suspected seizures

must_include: Intellectual disability is a neurodevelopmental condition starting in childhood, affecting learning and day-to-day functioning
Autosomal dominant inheritance means an affected parent can pass the condition to a child with ~50% risk (if a causative variant is identified)
Labs provided (CBC/CMP electrolytes/BUN) are generally not diagnostic; genetic evaluation is key to confirming cause
No single medication treats intellectual disability itself; care focuses on therapies, educational/vocational supports, and treating comorbid conditions
Plan for genetics referral/testing and coordinated multidisciplinary supports (speech/OT/PT/behavioral services)
Set expectations for follow-up to review results and adjust supports
Discuss prognosis in terms of functional goals and supports rather than cure
Offer genetic counseling for family members and discuss testing options
Review safety planning and capacity/guardianship considerations when relevant
Importance of structured routines, sleep hygiene, and consistent behavioral strategies
Encourage physical activity and balanced nutrition; address obesity risk if present
Ensure regular vision/hearing checks and preventive healthcare

Order targeted baseline evaluations only if clinically indicated (e.g., CBC/CMP/TSH, lead level, B12/folate) and/or tests to rule out reversible contributors; acknowledge that routine labs are generally not diagnostic for intellectual disability etiology.

Formulate working diagnosis of intellectual disability with suspected autosomal dominant genetic etiology; document differential (ASD, specific learning disorder, acquired brain injury, metabolic/endocrine causes) and rationale (onset in childhood, adaptive deficits).

Explain diagnosis, inheritance risk (~50% if a causative variant is identified), limitations of routine labs, need for genetic evaluation, prognosis framing around function/supports, and lifestyle/behavioral supports.

Arrange follow-up to review genetic testing/referral outcomes, coordinate multidisciplinary supports (speech/OT/PT/behavioral), address comorbidities, and revisit safety/capacity/guardianship planning as needed.
Close encounter after ensuring understanding, safety planning, and clear next steps.

nl_assertions: Agent must explicitly state that intellectual disability is a neurodevelopmental condition beginning in childhood that affects learning and day-to-day functioning (adaptive skills).

Agent must explain autosomal dominant inheritance and communicate ~50% recurrence risk to offspring when a causative variant is identified, and clarify that risk counseling depends on confirmed genetics.
Agent must state that CBC/CMP electrolytes/BUN are generally not diagnostic for the cause of intellectual disability and that genetic evaluation/testing is key to confirming etiology.

Agent must avoid implying there is a medication that treats intellectual disability itself; instead, focus on therapies, educational/vocational supports, and treatment of comorbid conditions (e.g., ADHD, anxiety, epilepsy, sleep problems) if present.
Agent must recommend genetics referral/testing and coordinated multidisciplinary supports (speech therapy, OT/PT, behavioral services) and specify follow-up to review results and adjust the plan.
Agent must address prognosis in terms of functional goals, supports, and quality of life rather than cure; avoid deterministic or stigmatizing language.
Agent must offer genetic counseling for the patient/family, discuss testing options for relatives when appropriate, and incorporate reproductive counseling considerations.
Agent must include safety planning and capacity/guardianship considerations when relevant (e.g., decision-making capacity, vulnerability, independent living safety, consent).

Agent must provide lifestyle/health maintenance education: structured routines, sleep hygiene, consistent behavioral strategies, physical activity and balanced nutrition (including obesity risk if relevant), and regular vision/hearing checks and preventive care.
If the agent prescribes any medication for comorbidities, it must perform CHECK_ALLERGY and contraindication screening before PRESCRIBE; failure is critical.

If the agent orders tests or procedures with contraindications (e.g., sedation, contrast imaging), it should perform contraindication screening before ordering; omission is acceptable but should be captured as a quality decrement (non-critical).
If the agent prescribes, it should perform CHECK_INTERACTION before PRESCRIBE when feasible; omission is a non-critical safety miss.

Agent should ask about red flags requiring escalation (new regression, seizures, focal neurologic deficits, safeguarding concerns/abuse, suicidality, uncontrolled aggression) and respond appropriately (urgent referral/emergency guidance) if present.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Primary hyperparathyroidism due to multigland (primary) parathyroid hyperplasia
required_evidence: Elevated serum calcium (total and/or ionized) on repeat testing
Inappropriately elevated or non-suppressed intact PTH in the setting of hypercalcemia
Serum phosphorus low or low-normal (PTH-mediated phosphaturia)
25-hydroxyvitamin D not severely deficient (or deficiency addressed) to avoid misclassification/secondary hyperparathyroidism
Creatinine/eGFR not consistent with advanced CKD as the primary driver of elevated PTH
Clinical context consistent with primary disease (e.g., no clear alternative cause such as malignancy with suppressed PTH)

expected_lab_results.Parathyroid hormone (intact PTH): Elevated or inappropriately normal (non-suppressed) despite hypercalcemia
expected_lab_results.25-hydroxyvitamin D: Often low-normal or mildly low; not profoundly low once repleted/assessed
expected_lab_results.Alkaline phosphatase: Normal to mildly elevated (may be higher if high bone turnover)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Normal or mildly elevated; not explaining PTH elevation (i.e., not advanced CKD pattern)

medications: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000-2000 IU PO daily (titrate to maintain 25-OH vitamin D >= 30 ng/mL)
Cinacalcet 30 mg PO twice daily (if not a surgical candidate or if persistent hypercalcemia; titrate every 2-4 weeks to calcium goal)
non_pharmacological: Refer to endocrine surgery for parathyroidectomy (preferred definitive treatment for primary hyperparathyroidism due to hyperplasia; plan for bilateral neck exploration/subtotal parathyroidectomy or total parathyroidectomy with autotransplant per surgeon/endocrine team)
Ensure adequate hydration; avoid dehydration
Maintain normal dietary calcium intake (avoid excessive restriction; avoid excessive supplementation unless directed)
Avoid medications that can worsen hypercalcemia if possible (e.g., thiazide diuretics, lithium); review current med list

Assess bone and renal complications and surgical criteria: DEXA, renal imaging for stones/nephrocalcinosis, and 24-hour urine calcium if available to help exclude familial hypocalciuric hypercalcemia and risk-stratify
If severe/symptomatic hypercalcemia occurs: urgent management with IV isotonic fluids and consider inpatient care; treat precipitants
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck calcium (total/ionized), phosphorus, creatinine, and PTH in 2-4 weeks after any medication changes (e.g., vitamin D repletion or cinacalcet initiation); if proceeding to surgery, pre-op endocrine/surgical visit within 2-6 weeks and post-op labs within 1-2 weeks, then at 3-6 months.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly states Basilicata-Akhtar syndrome is a suspected/working genetic diagnosis and that confirmatory genetic testing may be required.
Agent explains X-linked dominant inheritance in plain language, including typical family patterns and explicitly notes there is no male-to-male transmission.
Agent elicits a 3-generation family history and uses it to discuss implications for relatives and cascade testing.
Agent clearly communicates uncertainty and next steps (genetics referral, additional history, targeted evaluations).
Agent states there is no single curative medication and that care focuses on monitoring and treating manifestations.

Agent specifies which labs are being monitored (at minimum creatinine/eGFR, electrolytes, urinalysis and urine protein) and what findings trigger escalation (rising creatinine, declining eGFR, significant/progressive proteinuria, electrolyte abnormalities).
Before any prescribing, agent performs allergy_check and documents penicillin allergy details; failure is critical if prescribing occurs without this.

Before any prescribing, agent performs contraindication_check (e.g., pregnancy status for teratogens; renal artery stenosis history for ACEi/ARB; hyperkalemia risk; severe renal impairment dosing limits); failure is critical if prescribing occurs without this.
If drug_interaction_check is omitted, it is a non-critical miss, but if performed it must reference the patient’s current medication list and renal function.
If an ACE inhibitor/ARB is prescribed for proteinuria/HTN, agent includes renal-appropriate dosing considerations and a plan to recheck creatinine and potassium within 1-2 weeks.
Agent reviews kidney-protective lifestyle guidance, including avoiding unnecessary NSAIDs and discussing hydration and OTC/supplements.
Agent provides prognosis counseling framed around individual manifestations and surveillance rather than definitive outcomes.
Agent uses clear, empathetic, non-alarmist language and checks understanding (teach-back or equivalent).
Agent provides safety-net instructions for red-flag symptoms and defines follow-up timing based on severity.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start with: “My face and lips keep swelling up again, and my belly hurts really bad.” Early in the visit, volunteer: lips/cheeks swelling started a few hours ago; cramping belly pain in waves; mild nausea and no appetite; this has happened before and lasts a couple days.

If asked, add: no itchy rash/hives; tongue feels thick but you can swallow; voice is a bit hoarse; vomited once and had loose stools; allergy pills don’t help; sometimes hands/feet swell; prior scans were “normal.”

Do not mention unless specifically explored: triggers like stress/dental work/minor bumps; belly attacks without face swelling; throat tightness without wheeze; onset in teens; family member with similar “mystery swelling.”

Only if asked about meds/sex/hormones: say you started a birth control pill and attacks are more frequent; you’ve had painful genital swelling and avoided sex.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s food allergy, infection, anxiety, IBS/food poisoning. Emphasize cost fears: assume specialty meds are unaffordable, prefer prednisolone, worry about insurance/work, and think you must “save” meds. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fear, and ask what’s really happening and how to afford treatment.

medications: Prednisolone 20 mg PO daily (self-started for swelling; minimal benefit)
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
confounders: Estrogen-containing oral contraceptive use (can precipitate/worsen hereditary angioedema attacks)
Recent dental procedure 2 days ago (minor trauma trigger for facial/oropharyngeal swelling)
No urticaria/pruritus and normal tryptase (can lead clinicians to misclassify as allergic angioedema; steroids often ineffective)
Intermittent severe abdominal pain episodes in past (can mimic gastroenteritis/appendicitis and delay diagnosis)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hereditary angioedema (HAE) due to C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency/dysfunction (Type I or II)
acceptable_alternatives: Hereditary angioedema with normal C1 inhibitor (HAE-nl-C1INH)
Acquired angioedema due to C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency
required_evidence: Recurrent angioedema episodes without urticaria/pruritus and poor response to antihistamines/epinephrine/steroids (if previously tried)
Low C4 complement during or between attacks
Low C1-INH functional activity and/or low C1-INH antigenic level (Type I: low antigen + low function; Type II: normal/high antigen + low function)
C1q complement normal supports hereditary (low C1q suggests acquired angioedema)
Serum tryptase not elevated supports non–mast cell-mediated process

expected_lab_results.C4 complement: Low (typically below reference range), often persistently low even between attacks
expected_lab_results.C1 esterase inhibitor (C1-INH) antigenic: Type I: low; Type II: normal or elevated (if Type II, antigen may be normal/high)
expected_lab_results.C1 esterase inhibitor functional activit: Low (reduced functional activity is key abnormality)
expected_lab_results.C1q complement: Normal (helps distinguish hereditary from acquired forms where C1q is often low)
expected_lab_results.Serum tryptase: Normal (not elevated unless concurrent anaphylaxis/mast cell activation)
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Usually normal (may show mild stress leukocytosis during severe attacks)

medications: C1 esterase inhibitor (human) 20 units/kg IV once for acute attack (repeat per product guidance/clinical response)
Icatibant 30 mg SC once for acute attack (may repeat every 6 hours as needed; max 3 doses/24 hours)
Ecallantide 30 mg SC once for acute attack (administer by healthcare professional with anaphylaxis monitoring)
Fresh frozen plasma 2 units IV once if specific HAE therapies unavailable (use with caution; may worsen symptoms in some cases)
Discontinue Prednisolone for HAE attack treatment (ineffective for bradykinin-mediated angioedema) unless another indication exists
non_pharmacological: If any laryngeal involvement/voice change/stridor: treat as airway emergency—early airway assessment, prepare for intubation; ICU/ED management
Avoid known triggers (trauma, stress); avoid estrogen-containing contraceptives and ACE inhibitors if applicable
Provide/arrange access to on-demand therapy for future attacks and an emergency action plan
Consider long-term prophylaxis if frequent/severe attacks: lanadelumab SC, berotralstat PO, or regular C1-INH prophylaxis (specialist-directed)
Genetic/family counseling and screening of first-degree relatives where appropriate
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Urgent follow-up within 1–2 weeks with Allergy/Immunology (or HAE specialist) after stabilization; same-day/ED referral immediately for airway symptoms

must_include: HAE is typically bradykinin-mediated angioedema (not an allergy) and usually occurs without hives
Why C4 and C1-INH testing supports the diagnosis; role of C1q in distinguishing hereditary vs acquired
Red flags requiring emergency care: throat tightness, voice change, trouble breathing, rapid tongue swelling
Acute (on-demand) options: C1-INH, icatibant, ecallantide; when and how they are used
Steroids/antihistamines/epinephrine generally do not treat HAE swelling (unless allergic reaction is also suspected)
Airway-first approach for facial/oropharyngeal symptoms; need for ED/ICU if airway risk
Discuss expected course (swelling can progress over hours and last 2–5 days without targeted therapy)
Discuss medication access, self-administration plans (if applicable), and what to do if treatment fails
Review penicillin allergy history and reassure that standard HAE therapies are unrelated, while still documenting allergy
Trigger avoidance and medication avoidance (ACE inhibitors, estrogen-containing therapies) if relevant
Need for a written emergency plan and informing family/partners/work/school
Importance of specialist follow-up and considering prophylaxis if attacks are frequent/severe

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 28-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “My pee has looked kind of reddish-brown and I’m getting puffy around my eyes.” Early in the visit, volunteer: reddish-brown urine since yesterday, puffy eyelids in the mornings, mild ankle swelling by day’s end, and feeling more tired this week.

If asked about urinary changes, add: peeing less than usual and sometimes foamy urine. If asked about pain or other symptoms, mention a dull lower back/flank ache, headaches for 2 days, ~4 lb weight gain in a few days, and a sore throat 2 weeks ago that resolved.

Only if specifically asked, disclose: a pharmacy BP reading was high, you’ve been taking ibuprofen several times daily, urine looks darker after resting (not exercise), and you get short of breath on stairs.

Be hesitant to admit you took leftover antibiotics for 2 days; reveal only if directly asked about meds.

Express misconceptions: you worried about a UTI, muscle breakdown from a new workout, or “periodic fever.” If clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more open. Raise cost concerns and ask to minimize tests and use generics.

medications: Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO twice daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain (recent use)
confounders: Recent NSAID use (can worsen kidney function and cause interstitial nephritis)
Recent upper respiratory infection 3 weeks ago (raises consideration of post-infectious GN/IgA nephropathy)
Menses at time of urine collection (possible contamination contributing to hematuria signal)

required_evidence: Urinalysis positive for blood (hematuria)
Urine microscopy shows dysmorphic RBCs and/or RBC casts
Proteinuria present (typically sub-nephrotic; elevated urine protein/creatinine ratio)
Reduced eGFR and/or elevated serum creatinine/BUN consistent with glomerular inflammation

expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: Elevated from baseline (acute kidney injury pattern possible)
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - Protein: Positive (mild to moderate; usually not nephrotic range)
expected_lab_results.Urine protein/creatinine ratio: Elevated (commonly <3.5 g/g; sub-nephrotic typical for nephritic syndrome)

medications: Furosemide 20-40 mg PO daily (or BID) titrate to euvolemia if volume overloaded
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily if hypertensive (titrate as needed)
non_pharmacological: Salt restriction (e.g., <2 g sodium/day) and fluid restriction if edematous/volume overloaded
Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, iodinated contrast when possible) and dose-adjust renally cleared medications
Prompt nephrology referral for suspected glomerulonephritis; consider kidney biopsy depending on clinical course and severity
Monitor daily weights and blood pressure; assess for edema and reduced urine output
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck renal function and urinalysis within 48-72 hours (earlier if worsening symptoms), and urgent nephrology follow-up within 1 week

must_include: Findings suggest inflammation of the kidney filters (glomeruli) causing blood and protein in urine
RBC casts are a key sign that bleeding is coming from the kidney (not the bladder)
Kidney function appears reduced based on creatinine/BUN/eGFR
Treatment focuses on controlling blood pressure and fluid overload while determining the cause
Avoid NSAIDs and other kidney-stressing medicines
Need for close monitoring and nephrology evaluation; biopsy may be needed depending on severity
Review penicillin allergy and avoid penicillin-class antibiotics unless formally evaluated
Red flags requiring urgent care: worsening swelling, shortness of breath, very low urine output, severe headache/very high BP
Low-salt diet and fluid guidance if swelling is present
Home blood pressure monitoring and daily weights
Importance of follow-up labs to ensure kidney function is improving

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key nephritis history (hematuria/proteinuria symptoms, edema, urine output, HTN symptoms), timeline, triggers (recent infection), systemic features, pregnancy status if relevant, and current meds (esp. NSAIDs) to guide urgency and differential.

Order baseline nephritic workup and severity assessment: UA with microscopy (RBC casts), urine protein quantification, CMP/creatinine/eGFR, BUN, electrolytes, CBC, albumin, lipid panel if heavy protein, complements (C3/C4), ASO/anti-DNase B if post-strep suspected, ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, hepatitis B/C, HIV; consider renal ultrasound if obstruction/size concern.
Confirm nephritic pattern (hematuria with dysmorphic RBCs/RBC casts, proteinuria) and quantify renal dysfunction (creatinine/BUN/eGFR), plus assess for complications (hyperkalemia, acidosis).
Synthesize findings to diagnose nephritis/glomerulonephritis and stratify severity/need for urgent nephrology evaluation and possible biopsy.
Critical safety check before any prescribing: verify penicillin allergy details (reaction type, severity, timing) and document to avoid unsafe antibiotic selection.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions with planned BP/diuretic/ACEi/ARB or other meds, and adjust accordingly.

Initiate supportive treatment appropriate to nephritis severity (e.g., BP control, diuretic for edema if indicated, avoid nephrotoxins). Ensure contraindications are checked and renal dosing considered.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and key findings (RBC casts, reduced kidney function), explain treatment approach and avoidance of NSAIDs, address penicillin allergy and red flags, and provide lifestyle guidance (low salt, fluid guidance, BP/weights, follow-up labs).

Arrange close follow-up with repeat labs and nephrology evaluation; urgency based on renal function decline, BP, potassium, oliguria, or systemic features; discuss possible biopsy depending on severity.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and ensuring follow-up plan is set.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 46-year-old anxious, tangential project coordinator. Open with: “I keep waking up in the middle of the night and can’t get back to sleep.” Early on, volunteer: you wake 2–4 times nightly, are wide awake 30–90 minutes for months, feel tired/foggy, and are more irritable. Ramble about work stress and schedule.

If asked, explain you fall asleep okay but wake around 2–4 AM; your mind races, you check the clock, then stress; you nap late afternoons; you drink afternoon coffee and sometimes an energy drink; you use your phone in bed; OTC sleep aids help a bit but cause morning grogginess.

Do NOT volunteer: cannabis gummies, feeling down/on edge, or taking a friend’s sleeping pill—minimize or dodge unless asked directly; then admit reluctantly and ask not to document it.

Only reveal hidden issues if prompted: nocturia, nighttime heartburn (worse with late meals), wine most evenings (worse sleep), weight gain/dry mouth, weekend sleep-ins.

Express misconceptions: “Maybe perimenopause,” “thyroid,” or “low blood sugar.” Worry about costs of sleep studies, CBT-I, and prescriptions. If the clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and share more details.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
Melatonin 3 mg PO at bedtime as needed
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Perimenopausal hot flashes/night sweats causing nocturnal awakenings
Generalized anxiety disorder with nighttime rumination
SSRI-associated sleep fragmentation (sertraline)
Possible restless legs syndrome suggested by low ferritin
High caffeine intake in afternoon (self-reported)
Occasional alcohol use in evening to aid sleep (sleep fragmentation)
Possible obstructive sleep apnea risk due to overweight and snoring (not yet evaluated)

acceptable_alternatives: Psychophysiologic insomnia (if conditioned arousal features are elicited)
Insomnia due to another medical/psychiatric condition (only if supporting history present)
required_evidence: Difficulty maintaining sleep (frequent awakenings and/or early morning awakening) despite adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Daytime impairment attributable to poor sleep (e.g., fatigue, sleepiness, impaired concentration, mood disturbance, reduced performance)
Occurs at least 3 nights per week
Duration at least 3 months (for chronic insomnia); if <3 months then classify as short-term insomnia disorder
Not better explained by another sleep-wake disorder (e.g., OSA, restless legs syndrome), substance/medication effect, or untreated medical/psychiatric condition

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (no evidence of hyperthyroidism driving insomnia)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range or mildly elevated without symptomatic hyperglycemia; not explanatory of insomnia
expected_lab_results.Fasting glucose: Within reference range or mildly elevated; not explanatory of insomnia
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal (>=50 ng/mL) to reduce likelihood of iron-deficiency–associated restless legs contributing to awakenings
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Within reference range (no anemia contributing to fatigue/sleep disruption)
expected_lab_results.Vitamin D (25-OH): May be low or insufficient; not a primary cause of maintenance insomnia
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: Within reference range (no hyponatremia; important baseline if considering agents associated with SIADH in some cases)

medications: Doxepin 3 mg PO nightly 30 minutes before bed (for sleep maintenance; may increase to 6 mg nightly if needed)
Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly 1-2 hours before bedtime (optional adjunct if circadian contribution suspected; avoid high doses)
non_pharmacological: CBT-I as first-line (stimulus control, sleep restriction/bedtime window, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training)

Sleep hygiene: consistent wake time daily, avoid naps, limit caffeine after noon, avoid alcohol near bedtime, reduce evening fluids, wind-down routine, keep bedroom dark/quiet/cool, use bed only for sleep/sex

Screen for and address contributors to maintenance insomnia: nocturia, pain, GERD, anxiety/depression, medication/substance use, and evaluate for OSA/RLS symptoms; refer for sleep study if high OSA risk
Sleep diary for 1-2 weeks to guide CBT-I and track progress
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks to review sleep diary, CBT-I adherence/response, side effects, and need for medication adjustment; earlier if adverse effects occur

must_include: Define insomnia disorder and specifically sleep-maintenance insomnia
Confirm it is based on symptoms plus daytime impact and persistence/frequency criteria
Explain that labs help rule out contributing conditions (e.g., thyroid disease, iron deficiency) but insomnia is often maintained by behavioral/physiologic arousal patterns
CBT-I is first-line and most durable treatment; medication is adjunct/short-term support when needed
How to take doxepin (low-dose, bedtime timing) and expected benefits (fewer awakenings/longer sleep maintenance)
Key risks: next-day sedation, dizziness, anticholinergic effects at higher doses; avoid alcohol/other sedatives; do not drive if impaired
Set expectations: improvement over weeks; track with sleep diary; avoid escalating doses without follow-up
Acknowledge and document allergies: penicillin (hives) and diphenhydramine (paradoxical agitation); avoid recommending sedating antihistamines for sleep
Discuss when to seek help urgently (severe confusion, falls, suicidal thoughts, allergic reaction symptoms)
Shared decision-making about medication vs CBT-I-only approach and patient preferences
Stimulus control instructions (leave bed if awake ~20 minutes, return when sleepy; bed reserved for sleep/sex)
Sleep restriction concept (temporarily limit time in bed to consolidate sleep, then gradually expand)
Caffeine/alcohol/nicotine guidance and evening light/screen exposure reduction
Consistent wake time as the anchor behavior

Pain worsened by sitting and improved by standing/lying (positional feature typical of pudendal neuralgia)
Pain localized to pudendal nerve territory (vulva/perineum) without primary infectious dermatologic lesions on exam
No systemic inflammatory signs on basic labs (CRP/ESR not elevated) supporting non-inflammatory neuropathic etiology
Conjunctival hyperemia recognized as likely unrelated comorbidity/red-flag symptom requiring separate evaluation (e.g., dry eye/allergy/uveitis) rather than explaining vulvar pain

expected_lab_results.CBC (Hemoglobin): Within reference range (no anemia driving pain/fatigue)
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel (Creatinine): Normal renal function (suitable for gabapentinoids/duloxetine dosing as applicable)
expected_lab_results.Basic metabolic panel (Glucose, fasting): Normal or mildly elevated; not diagnostic for neuropathic pain
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal or mildly elevated; screen for diabetes-related neuropathy contribution
expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (exclude hypothyroidism as contributor to neuropathic symptoms)

medications: Gabapentin 100 mg PO at bedtime; increase by 100 mg every 3-7 days as tolerated toward 300 mg PO three times daily (max per tolerance/response)
Lidocaine 5% ointment apply thin layer to painful vulvar area nightly or 20-30 minutes before provocation (e.g., sitting/intercourse) as needed
non_pharmacological: Pelvic floor physical therapy with focus on down-training/relaxation and myofascial release (avoid Kegel strengthening initially if hypertonic)
Activity modification: minimize prolonged sitting; use cut-out (U-shaped) cushion; frequent standing breaks
Avoid vulvar irritants (scented soaps, douches, tight clothing); use gentle emollients as needed
Consider referral to pelvic pain specialist for diagnostic/therapeutic pudendal nerve block if inadequate response
Screen and treat comorbid mood/sleep issues; consider CBT-based pain coping strategies
Separate evaluation of conjunctival hyperemia: assess for allergy/dry eye; urgent ophthalmology if pain, photophobia, decreased vision, or unilateral severe redness (uveitis/keratitis red flags)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks for pain control, functional improvement, and medication tolerability; earlier (1-2 weeks) if side effects or worsening symptoms

must_include: Explain pudendal neuralgia as neuropathic pain from irritation/entrapment of the pudendal nerve causing vulvar/perineal burning pain
Clarify that routine labs are often normal and that diagnosis is clinical after excluding infection/dermatologic causes
Acknowledge conjunctival hyperemia may be a separate issue and outline when eye symptoms need urgent evaluation (pain, photophobia, vision change)
Set expectations: gradual improvement over weeks; aim is reduced pain and improved function
Review gabapentin titration, common side effects (sedation, dizziness), and cautions (driving/falls, alcohol)
Explain topical lidocaine use and potential local irritation/numbness; avoid applying to broken skin
Elicit patient goals and triggers (sitting, intercourse, cycling) and validate pain experience
Discuss sexual pain impact and offer options (timing lidocaine, pelvic PT, lubricants, referral as needed)
Provide red flags requiring prompt reassessment: new neurologic deficits, bowel/bladder dysfunction, fever, rapidly worsening pain
Sitting modification strategies (cut-out cushion, posture changes, breaks)
Vulvar skin care and avoidance of irritants
Importance of pelvic floor PT adherence and home relaxation exercises

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for pudendal neuralgia and exclude urgent/emergent causes (neurologic deficits, bowel/bladder dysfunction, infection, malignancy), plus clarify eye symptom details (pain/photophobia/vision change).

Assess triggers and functional impact (sitting, cycling, intercourse), prior evaluations/treatments, and patient goals; screen for pregnancy possibility and comorbidities relevant to medication choice.

If clinically indicated, order targeted tests to exclude infection/dermatologic causes (e.g., urinalysis, STI testing, vaginal swab) rather than routine broad labs; do not overtest when presentation is classic and prior workup negative.
Review ordered test results and integrate into differential; normal/negative results support a clinical diagnosis after exclusion of other causes.
Make a clinical diagnosis of pudendal neuralgia (neuropathic vulvar/perineal pain consistent with pudendal nerve irritation/entrapment) and document excluded alternatives and absence of red flags.
Required safety check before prescribing gabapentin and/or topical lidocaine.
Check interactions (non-critical) before prescribing, especially with other sedatives, opioids, alcohol use, and CNS depressants.

Collect contraindication-relevant history before prescribing (critical): renal disease for gabapentin dosing, history of severe lidocaine reactions, cardiac conduction issues if large-area topical anesthetic use, pregnancy/breastfeeding context, fall risk.
Initiate evidence-based symptomatic management (e.g., gabapentin titration; topical lidocaine) with clear dosing/titration and safety counseling.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment expectations and side effects, address sexual pain and patient concerns, provide lifestyle guidance (sitting modifications, vulvar care, pelvic floor PT).
Arrange follow-up to assess response/tolerability (e.g., 2–6 weeks) and earlier reassessment for red flags; consider referrals (pelvic floor PT, pain specialist, gynecology/urology) if refractory.
Close the encounter after ensuring understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

Agent explicitly checks for red flags and escalates appropriately: new saddle anesthesia or focal weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction/retention/incontinence, fever/systemic illness, rapidly worsening pain, unexplained weight loss, history of cancer, or trauma.

Agent differentiates pudendal neuralgia from common mimics by asking about and/or excluding: vulvovaginal infection, dermatologic conditions (lichen sclerosus), interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome, endometriosis, pelvic mass, and musculoskeletal pelvic floor spasm.
Agent explains pudendal neuralgia as neuropathic pain from irritation/entrapment of the pudendal nerve causing vulvar/perineal burning pain.
Agent clarifies that routine labs are often normal and diagnosis is clinical after excluding infection/dermatologic causes; avoids claiming a definitive lab confirmation.

Before prescribing, agent performs required safety checks: allergy_check (critical) and contraindication_check (critical) and documents completion; interaction check is performed or explicitly justified if omitted.

Agent provides a safe gabapentin plan: start low and titrate gradually; counsels on sedation/dizziness, driving/falls risk, and avoiding alcohol/CNS depressants; considers renal impairment for dosing.
Agent explains topical lidocaine use: how/when to apply, expected numbness/local irritation, and to avoid applying to broken/irritated skin or occluding large areas.
Agent elicits patient goals and validates pain experience; explores triggers (sitting, intercourse, cycling) and functional impact.
Agent addresses sexual pain impact and offers options (timing lidocaine, pelvic floor PT, lubricants, and referral as needed) using sensitive, nonjudgmental language.

Agent provides lifestyle education: sitting modifications (cut-out cushion, posture changes, breaks), vulvar skin care/avoid irritants, and importance of pelvic floor PT adherence and home relaxation exercises.
Agent safety-nets with clear return precautions and arranges follow-up within an appropriate timeframe to reassess efficacy and adverse effects.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 19-year-old male who speaks vaguely and is a bit evasive. Start with: “I keep getting more unsteady on my feet, worsening over the past couple years.” Volunteer only: wobbly “like I’m drunk,” bumping doorways, stairs feel unsafe, occasional slurred speech when tired, a few falls in 6 months, and that doctors said your ankle reflexes are weak/missing.

If asked directly, add: clumsy/shaky hands with fine tasks, overshooting reaches, occasional spinning (worse in dark/quick turns), numb/tingly feet at night, lower-leg weakness and smaller calves, worse night vision/light sensitivity, thirst/urination, unintentional weight loss, cramps, fatigue.

Keep hidden unless specifically asked: choking on thin liquids, needing to watch your feet on uneven ground, erectile difficulty, “borderline” sugars, hearing trouble.

Resist/deny at first: urinary accidents, social avoidance, anxiety/depression; admit only with gentle, nonjudgmental questioning.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s anemia (ice craving), vitamins (mouth sores/sore tongue), “genetic syndrome” (skin bumps), or like your uncle’s inherited ataxia.

When empathy is shown, soften and share more, but emphasize fear of costs, losing your job, and genetic info reaching employers/insurers.

medications: Insulin glargine 18 units subcutaneous nightly
Insulin lispro 6 units subcutaneous with meals (adjust per carbohydrate intake)
Lisinopril 5 mg PO daily
Vincristine 2 mg IV weekly (chemotherapy protocol)
confounders: Vincristine-induced peripheral neuropathy contributing to decreased/absent ankle reflexes and gait instability
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy (early) contributing to reduced ankle reflexes and sensory symptoms
Borderline functional vitamin B12 deficiency (elevated methylmalonic acid) that can mimic or worsen neuropathy/ataxia
Visual impairment from retinitis pigmentosa affecting balance and gait assessment

differentials: infantile-onset autosomal recessive nonprogressive cerebellar ataxia

required_evidence: Progressive cerebellar ataxia phenotype (worsening gait/limb ataxia and/or dysarthria over time)
X-linked inheritance pattern suggested by family history (affected males, maternal transmission) or confirmatory genetic testing consistent with an X-linked ataxia syndrome
Peripheral neuropathy features consistent with decreased/absent ankle reflexes

Exclusion of key mimics based on history/exam and targeted testing (e.g., no mucocutaneous lesions or cancer predisposition suggesting Cowden; no anemia features suggesting X-linked sideroblastic anemia; nonprogressive course would argue for infantile-onset AR nonprogressive ataxia)

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Typically normal (no diabetes-specific pattern expected solely from X-linked progressive cerebellar ataxia); may be elevated if vincristine-associated dysglycemia or comorbid diabetes is present
expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Typically normal; monitor for hyperglycemia if clinically indicated

medications: No disease-modifying pharmacotherapy established for X-linked progressive cerebellar ataxia; avoid neurotoxic agents when possible (including vincristine if alternatives exist)
non_pharmacological: Neurology referral for confirmation (including genetic counseling/testing) and longitudinal management
Physical therapy focused on gait/balance training and fall-prevention strategies
Occupational therapy for adaptive equipment and activities of daily living support
Speech-language pathology for dysarthria and swallowing screening if symptomatic
Assistive device assessment (cane/walker) and home safety evaluation to reduce fall risk
Screen for and manage peripheral neuropathy symptoms; document reflexes and sensory findings over time
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 4-8 weeks after diagnostic workup initiation; sooner (1-2 weeks) if rapid progression, frequent falls, or new bulbar symptoms

must_include: The condition affects the cerebellum and can worsen over time, causing coordination and balance problems
The decreased/absent ankle reflexes can reflect associated peripheral nerve involvement
Rationale for considering an X-linked condition and the role of genetic testing and family history
Why certain differentials are less likely given the course and available findings
No proven cure; management focuses on symptom control, maintaining function, and preventing complications (falls, aspiration)
Key role of PT/OT/SLP and assistive devices
Medication review emphasizing avoidance/minimization of neurotoxic agents; discuss vincristine as a potential contributor to neuropathy and need to coordinate with oncology/hematology if applicable
Discuss prognosis as variable and typically progressive; emphasize monitoring and supportive care benefits
Offer genetic counseling for patient and family (inheritance, reproductive implications)
Safety planning for falls and when to seek urgent care (new severe weakness, sudden worsening, choking/aspiration signs)
Fall-prevention strategies (remove tripping hazards, adequate lighting, footwear, assistive device use)
Avoid alcohol excess and sedating medications that worsen ataxia
Encourage regular supervised exercise/therapy program and adherence to follow-up
If swallowing issues arise, follow diet/texture recommendations and aspiration precautions

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit onset/course of ataxia, progression, triggers, associated symptoms (neuropathy, dysarthria, dysphagia), functional impact, and red flags to frame differential and urgency.
Clarify family history with emphasis on maternal lineage, affected males, miscarriages, and pattern consistent with X-linked inheritance; ask about consanguinity and ethnicity if relevant.
Medication/substance review (alcohol, sedatives, antiepileptics, chemo agents such as vincristine), toxin exposure, and comorbidities to identify iatrogenic/toxic contributors and modifiable risks.

Order appropriate baseline evaluation to exclude treatable mimics and assess neuropathy contributors (e.g., CBC/CMP, TSH, B12/E, copper/ceruloplasmin if indicated, HbA1c, SPEP/IFE as appropriate for neuropathy/ataxia workup).
Interpret results to support progressive inherited ataxia vs acquired causes; document why key reversible etiologies are less likely if normal.
Synthesize progressive cerebellar ataxia with peripheral neuropathy features and X-linked pattern; state working diagnosis and differentials; recommend genetic testing and genetic counseling.

Explain diagnosis in patient-centered language including cerebellar involvement, progression, neuropathy/reflex findings, rationale for X-linked consideration, and why alternative diagnoses are less likely.

Explain treatment approach: no cure, symptom management, PT/OT/SLP, assistive devices, fall/aspiration prevention, medication review and avoidance of neurotoxic/sedating agents; discuss vincristine neuropathy risk and coordination with oncology/hematology if applicable.
Required safety check before prescribing any medication.
Non-critical but expected check to reduce harm when prescribing symptomatic therapies in a neurologic condition with polypharmacy risk.

If symptomatic medications are indicated, prescribe cautiously after safety checks (e.g., address spasticity, tremor, neuropathic pain) and avoid agents that worsen ataxia/sedation; otherwise document no meds started.

Address concerns: prognosis variability and typical progression, supportive care benefits, genetic counseling/reproductive implications, safety plan and urgent return precautions (sudden worsening, new severe weakness, choking/aspiration).
Lifestyle education: fall prevention, avoid alcohol excess and sedating meds, supervised exercise/therapy adherence, swallowing/aspiration precautions and diet/texture guidance if dysphagia emerges.
Arrange neurology follow-up, referrals (PT/OT/SLP, genetics), and monitoring plan (falls, swallowing, nutrition, progression).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions, and plan.

must_include: AIP is a rapidly progressive inflammatory injury of the lungs causing severe low oxygen, similar to ARDS
Diagnosis is made by pattern plus excluding other causes like infection and heart failure

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit time course and severity of dyspnea/cough/fever, hypoxemia symptoms, exposures, aspiration, recent illness, travel, sick contacts, medications, immune status, and red flags to frame AIP vs infection/PE/CHF and determine urgency.

Order initial evaluation to assess gas exchange and exclude mimics: ABG/VBG, CBC, CMP, CRP/ESR, lactate, procalcitonin, blood cultures, sputum culture, respiratory viral PCR, BNP, troponin as indicated, coagulation panel; consider autoimmune serologies if suggested by history.
Interpret results for hypoxemic respiratory failure and evidence for/against infection, cardiogenic edema, or other systemic causes.

Order imaging and cardiopulmonary exclusion tests: CXR and CT chest (HRCT) if feasible; ECG; consider echocardiography to assess cardiac function/volume overload; consider D-dimer/CTPA if PE risk is significant.
Confirm diffuse bilateral infiltrates/ground-glass pattern compatible with ARDS/AIP and ensure no clear alternative explanation (e.g., focal lobar pneumonia, cardiogenic pulmonary edema).

Make a working diagnosis of acute interstitial pneumonia when rapid ARDS-like pattern is present and other causes (infection, heart failure, PE, known ILD exacerbation) are reasonably excluded; document differential and uncertainty.
Perform required critical allergy check (including penicillin allergy details) before any antimicrobial or other prescribing.
Check for clinically relevant drug-drug interactions for planned therapies (e.g., steroids, empiric antibiotics, anticoagulation prophylaxis), even if non-critical.

Initiate treatment plan prioritizing oxygenation/ventilatory support escalation; consider empiric non-penicillin antibiotics if infection not excluded; consider systemic corticosteroids after infection evaluation; include VTE prophylaxis if not contraindicated and bleeding risk assessed.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment/supportive ICU measures, address prognosis/ICU-intubation concerns and medication risks, confirm/avoid penicillin-class antibiotics, provide lifestyle and return precautions.
Arrange appropriate disposition and follow-up: ICU/step-down for hypoxemia; outpatient pulmonology follow-up only if clinically stable; plan for repeat imaging/PFTs after recovery.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks completed, treatment initiated, and clear escalation/return precautions provided.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 32-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I get out of breath really easily, even just walking around the house.” Early in the visit, volunteer: shortness of breath with small activities (stairs/groceries), unusual fatigue, occasional racing heartbeat with walking, and ankle puffiness by day’s end. If asked about associated symptoms, share: lightheadedness with standing/fast walking, exertional chest tightness/pressure that improves with rest, waking at night short of breath needing to sit up, and declining walking distance over months. Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: needing more pillows to sleep, bluish lips/fingertips when very winded, and a few pounds of weight gain without eating more. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it, relax, and become more willing to share. tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for secretory diarrhea syndrome and severity: duration, volume (nocturnal/fasting persistence), dehydration symptoms, weight loss, abdominal pain, flushing, medication/laxative use, infection risks, endocrine history, prior imaging, and red flags (syncope, palpitations, oliguria, confusion).

Order initial stabilization/diagnostic labs: BMP (Na/K/Cl/HCO3/Cr), Mg, phosphate, glucose, CBC, VBG/ABG if ill, stool electrolytes/osmolality (to support secretory diarrhea), and fasting VIP level; consider LFTs and calcium as part of NET workup.
Interpret results for VIPoma pattern: hypokalemia, non–anion gap metabolic acidosis (low bicarbonate), dehydration/AKI, and markedly elevated fasting VIP level supporting diagnosis.

Synthesize clinical + lab pattern to diagnose suspected/confirmed VIPoma causing WDHA (watery diarrhea, hypokalemia, achlorhydria/acid-base disturbance) and document severity/need for urgent correction.
Critical safety check before prescribing (e.g., octreotide, antiemetics, electrolyte replacements) to avoid allergic reactions.
Noncritical safety check before prescribing to identify interactions (e.g., octreotide effects with diabetes meds, QT-prolonging agents if severe electrolyte derangements).

Initiate treatment: IV isotonic fluids and electrolyte repletion (K and Mg) and start somatostatin analog (octreotide) to reduce VIP secretion/diarrhea; ensure dosing/route appropriate to acuity and arrange inpatient vs urgent management if unstable.

Critical contraindication check before ordering any contrast imaging or procedures (e.g., renal impairment for contrast studies) and order staging workup: CT/MRI pancreas, somatostatin receptor imaging (e.g., Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT) and additional NET labs as indicated.
Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, immediate priorities, octreotide benefits/risks, red flags, hydration/electrolyte plan, and expectations for staging and definitive therapy.
Arrange close follow-up with endocrinology/oncology/surgery; plan monitoring of electrolytes, renal function, glucose, and gallbladder issues with longer-term octreotide.
Close encounter after safety netting and confirming understanding.

nl_assertions: Agent must recognize a secretory diarrhea syndrome (large-volume watery diarrhea that persists with fasting/nocturnally) and prioritize stabilization over definitive tumor workup when dehydration/electrolyte derangements are suspected.

Agent must explicitly assess for severity/red flags (weakness, palpitations, confusion, decreased urination, dizziness/syncope) and advise urgent/emergent care if present or if unable to maintain hydration.

Agent must order and correctly interpret key labs: potassium, bicarbonate/acid-base status, magnesium, renal function, and a fasting VIP level; must state that a markedly elevated fasting VIP supports the diagnosis in the right clinical context.

Agent must not diagnose VIPoma without addressing alternative causes of watery diarrhea (infection, medication/laxatives, endocrine causes, malabsorption) and must document why VIPoma is favored based on pattern and VIP level.
Before any prescribing, agent must perform allergy_check (critical) and contraindication_check (critical); omission is a critical failure.
Before ordering contrast imaging or other potentially contraindicated tests, agent must perform contraindication_check (critical); omission is a critical failure.

If octreotide is recommended, agent must explain its role (reduces VIP secretion, often rapid improvement) and mention key adverse effects and monitoring (abdominal cramps, gallstones with long-term use, glucose changes).

Agent must include immediate treatment priorities: IV fluids plus potassium/magnesium repletion and correction of acid-base abnormalities; must avoid unsafe advice such as anti-motility agents as sole therapy in severe illness or delaying electrolyte correction.

Agent must communicate definitive management depends on staging and must mention surgery for localized disease and additional options for metastatic disease (medical/interventional) without overpromising cure.

Lifestyle counseling must include hydration/electrolyte instructions, avoiding OTC laxatives, tracking stool volume/frequency and weight, and clear return precautions for worsening diarrhea or electrolyte imbalance symptoms.

minimal_paths.description: Patient with classic WDHA features; labs show hypokalemia, metabolic acidosis, dehydration and markedly elevated fasting VIP; agent stabilizes, starts octreotide, and initiates staging.
must_collect: large-volume watery diarrhea (often >3 L/day) and persistence with fasting/nocturnal diarrhea
signs/symptoms of dehydration (thirst, dizziness, decreased urination)
weight loss and duration of symptoms
comorbidities impacting therapy (diabetes, renal disease) and current meds
steps: ASK: characterize diarrhea and severity; screen red flags and laxative/medication causes
ORDER_LAB: BMP, Mg, renal function, acid-base assessment, stool studies as available, fasting VIP
GET_RESULTS: identify hypokalemia, low bicarbonate/non–anion gap acidosis, AKI/dehydration, markedly elevated VIP
DIAGNOSE: VIPoma causing secretory diarrhea (WDHA syndrome), document severity
PRESCRIBE: IV fluids + K/Mg repletion; start octreotide; plan monitoring of electrolytes/glucose
contraindication_check before ordering staging imaging; ORDER_LAB/diagnostics for staging (CT/MRI pancreas, SSTR imaging)
EDUCATE: diagnosis, treatment priorities, octreotide benefits/risks, red flags, lifestyle guidance
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: urgent specialty referral and lab recheck plan

minimal_paths.description: Patient appears clinically unstable from dehydration/electrolyte loss; agent prioritizes emergent stabilization and does not delay fluids/electrolytes while initiating VIPoma evaluation.
must_collect: baseline kidney disease or heart failure (affects fluid strategy)
steps: ASK: rapid severity assessment and red flags; determine need for ED/inpatient care
ORDER_LAB: urgent electrolytes/renal function/acid-base and fasting VIP (if feasible)
GET_RESULTS: confirm severe electrolyte derangement/AKI and acid-base disturbance
DIAGNOSE: severe secretory diarrhea with high suspicion for VIPoma; note need for inpatient management
CHECK_ALLERGY + contraindication_check (+ CHECK_INTERACTION if time) before prescribing
PRESCRIBE: urgent IV fluids and aggressive K/Mg repletion; start octreotide when safe
EDUCATE: red flags and urgency; explain expected rapid symptom improvement with octreotide but longer staging process
SCHEDULE_FOLLOWUP: coordinate inpatient/specialty care and staging once stabilized

acceptable_variants.description: Agent orders stool osmotic gap/electrolytes and infectious stool testing early to support secretory diarrhea and exclude infection before finalizing VIPoma diagnosis, while still prioritizing stabilization.
steps: ORDER_LAB (BMP, Mg, VBG/ABG, stool electrolytes/osmolality, stool pathogen testing, fasting VIP)

acceptable_variants.description: Agent initiates octreotide after confirming hallmark electrolyte pattern and clinical secretory diarrhea, even before VIP result returns, while documenting rationale and monitoring plan.
steps: PRESCRIBE (IV fluids, K/Mg, octreotide; glucose monitoring)

profile.occupation: Administrative assistant at a small law office

patient.chief_complaint: My fingers keep turning white and going numb when I’m cold, and my hands feel tight and puffy.

volunteer: My fingers turn white or bluish in the cold or when I’m stressed, then throb as they warm up.
My hands feel puffy in the mornings and my skin feels tight, like it’s harder to bend my fingers.
I’ve been feeling more anxious lately, especially when my symptoms flare.
if_asked: I get bad heartburn and sometimes food feels like it’s sticking in my chest.
My fingertips get sore and I’ve had a couple of small cracks/sores that take a long time to heal.
My hands are extra sensitive to cold and can be painful even with mild chill.
I feel more tired than usual even when I sleep okay.
My wrists and fingers ache off and on.
hidden: I get short of breath going up stairs more than I used to, but I thought I was just out of shape.
I’ve had a dry cough that’s been hanging around for weeks.
My mouth feels tighter and it’s harder to open wide at the dentist, but I didn’t think it mattered.
My eyes and mouth feel dry a lot of the time, so I sip water constantly.
resistant: Sometimes I leak a little stool or have trouble holding in gas, especially when my stomach is acting up (I’m embarrassed to talk about it).
misleading: I looked it up online and thought it might be carpal tunnel because my hands get numb and tingly.
I wondered if it was just anxiety or panic because the color changes happen when I’m stressed.
noise: I’ve had a mild headache on and off this week.
My seasonal allergies have been acting up with sneezing and a runny nose.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about stomach symptoms, reflux, or trouble swallowing.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about skin changes, wounds, or finger pain.

progressive_reveal.symptom: I get short of breath going up stairs more than I used to, and I’ve had a dry cough that’s been hanging around.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about breathing, exercise tolerance, or cough.

progressive_reveal.symptom: Sometimes I leak a little stool or have trouble holding in gas.
progressive_reveal.trigger: Only revealed after reassurance and direct, nonjudgmental questions about bowel control or accidents.

general_concerns: I read that scleroderma is basically the same as lupus and that it always turns into kidney failure.
If I stop feeling anxious and reduce stress, the disease should go away on its own.
My skin feels tight, so I assumed moisturizing more will prevent the internal organ problems too.
Because it’s an autoimmune disease, I think I should avoid all vaccines since they might ‘overstimulate’ my immune system.
I’m worried that using medications that suppress the immune system will make me constantly sick and I won’t be able to be around my kids.
economic_concerns: Specialist visits and tests seem optional—if I feel okay, I can skip follow-ups to save money.
I’m considering spacing out or halving prescribed medications to make them last longer and reduce copays.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 44-year-old cooperative, clear-speaking administrative assistant. Start with: “My fingers keep turning white and going numb when I’m cold, and my hands feel tight and puffy.” Early in the visit, volunteer: fingers turn white/blue with cold or stress then throb on warming; morning puffiness and tight skin making it harder to bend fingers; increased anxiety when flares happen. If asked about other symptoms, mention: bad heartburn and food sticking; sore fingertips with small cracks/sores slow to heal; marked cold sensitivity and pain; fatigue; off-and-on wrist/finger aches. Do not volunteer unless directly asked: more shortness of breath on stairs, a weeks-long dry cough, tighter mouth/opening at dentist, frequent dry eyes/mouth. Only reveal if specifically asked about bowel control: occasional stool leakage or trouble holding gas; act embarrassed and hesitant. Express misconceptions: you wondered about carpal tunnel or anxiety/panic causing it. If clinician shows empathy, acknowledge and open up more. Bring up cost concerns: you may skip follow-ups, space out tests, or halve meds to save money.

medications: Amlodipine 5 mg PO daily
Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily
Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Hydroxychloroquine 200 mg PO twice daily
Albuterol HFA 90 mcg/inhalation: 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed
comorbidities: Iron deficiency (likely from chronic GI blood loss/poor intake)
confounders: Panic attacks causing episodic dyspnea/chest tightness that can mimic cardiopulmonary involvement (e.g., pulmonary arterial hypertension)
Mild intermittent asthma complicating evaluation of cough/wheeze vs interstitial lung disease
GERD with possible microaspiration contributing to chronic cough and worsening breathlessness
Borderline renal dysfunction and trace proteinuria complicating assessment for early scleroderma renal involvement vs dehydration/NSAID exposure
Iron deficiency anemia contributing to fatigue, palpitations, and exertional dyspnea

required_evidence: History of scleroderma/systemic sclerosis with current visit focused on symptom management rather than establishing new diagnosis
Anxiety symptoms temporally associated with chronic disease burden and/or uncertainty about prognosis
No CBC/BMP abnormalities suggesting an alternative acute medical cause for anxiety (e.g., severe infection, major electrolyte derangement)
Medication/allergy review to ensure symptoms are not due to adverse drug effects or withdrawal

expected_lab_results.CBC - Hemoglobin: Typically normal or mildly low; no acute anemia expected solely from anxiety
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Usually normal (normocytic); not expected to explain anxiety in isolation
expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal; no leukocytosis expected without infection/inflammation flare

medications: Sertraline 25 mg PO daily x 1 week, then 50 mg PO daily if tolerated
non_pharmacological: Screen severity and safety: GAD-7 and assess for suicidal ideation; evaluate functional impairment
Offer/referral to cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) for anxiety (first-line, especially with chronic illness)
Teach brief coping skills: diaphragmatic breathing, grounding techniques, sleep hygiene
Encourage regular low-impact exercise as tolerated and structured daily routine
Coordinate care with rheumatology; review scleroderma symptom control and triggers contributing to anxiety
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 2-4 weeks to assess response/tolerability; earlier (within 1 week) if symptoms worsen or any safety concerns

must_include: Anxiety is common in chronic illnesses like systemic sclerosis and is treatable
Review that current basic labs do not suggest an acute electrolyte or blood-count cause for anxiety (if results are normal)
Clarify goals: reduce worry/physical symptoms, improve function and sleep
SSRI (sertraline) is a first-line medication option; onset typically 2-4 weeks with maximal benefit by 6-8 weeks
Common side effects (GI upset, headache, sleep changes) and that starting low reduces side effects
Do not stop abruptly; discuss tapering if discontinuing
When to seek urgent help: suicidal thoughts, severe agitation, signs of serotonin syndrome (rare)
Elicit patient priorities and fears related to scleroderma and anxiety
Confirm allergies (TMP-SMX urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and reassure avoidance in care settings
Discuss pregnancy intentions if relevant (to guide medication choice) and substance use/caffeine that can worsen anxiety
Sleep hygiene and limiting caffeine/nicotine/alcohol to reduce anxiety symptoms
Stress-management plan (breathing exercises, mindfulness/CBT tools) and consistent physical activity as tolerated
Encourage ongoing scleroderma management and prompt reporting of new cardiopulmonary symptoms that can mimic anxiety (e.g., dyspnea, chest pain)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit anxiety symptoms, duration, triggers, functional impairment, sleep, panic symptoms; screen for suicidality/mania/psychosis; assess scleroderma-related cardiopulmonary symptoms that can mimic anxiety (dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations); review meds/substances (caffeine, nicotine, alcohol, stimulants) and pregnancy intentions.
Order basic labs to exclude common reversible contributors if not already available (e.g., CBC, CMP/electrolytes, TSH).
Confirm whether basic labs are normal to support reassurance that no acute electrolyte or blood-count cause is evident (if normal).
Make a working diagnosis of anxiety disorder (e.g., GAD) in the context of systemic sclerosis, while documenting red-flag symptom absence or plans to evaluate if present.
Critical safety check before prescribing; confirm and document allergies (TMP-SMX urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and ensure planned medication is not contraindicated by allergy.
Critical safety check before prescribing; assess interactions between sertraline and current medications (including OTC/herbals such as St. John’s wort, triptans, linezolid/MAOIs).
Initiate first-line pharmacotherapy option (sertraline) with low starting dose and titration plan; include counseling on onset, side effects, and tapering.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis; explain treatment and urgent warning signs; address concerns (priorities/fears, allergies, pregnancy intentions, substance/caffeine); educate lifestyle (sleep hygiene, stress-management/CBT tools, exercise as tolerated, and when to report cardiopulmonary symptoms).
Arrange follow-up to assess tolerability, suicidality, symptom response, and dose titration (typically 2–4 weeks), with earlier review if adverse effects or worsening symptoms.
Close the encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: The agent screens for immediate safety issues (suicidal ideation/self-harm, severe agitation, psychosis, mania/hypomania) before initiating SSRI therapy and provides an urgent help plan if positive.

The agent explicitly explains that anxiety is common in chronic illnesses like systemic sclerosis and is treatable, and clarifies patient-centered goals (reduce worry/physical symptoms, improve function and sleep).

If basic labs are normal/available, the agent states that current CBC/electrolytes do not suggest an acute blood-count or electrolyte cause for anxiety; if not available, the agent avoids claiming normality and instead orders/reviews them.

The agent elicits patient priorities and fears related to scleroderma and anxiety, and acknowledges the overlap between anxiety symptoms and cardiopulmonary symptoms that warrant evaluation (e.g., dyspnea, chest pain).
Before prescribing, the agent performs all required safety checks: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; failure of any critical check is benchmark failure.
The agent confirms and documents allergies (TMP-SMX urticarial rash; latex contact dermatitis) and reassures avoidance in care settings.
The agent discusses pregnancy intentions when relevant to medication choice and counseling, and reviews substance use and caffeine that can worsen anxiety.
When prescribing sertraline, the agent states it is a first-line option, explains onset (2–4 weeks) and maximal benefit (6–8 weeks), and recommends starting low to reduce side effects.
The agent counsels on common SSRI side effects (GI upset, headache, sleep changes), advises not to stop abruptly, and discusses tapering if discontinuing.
The agent provides safety-net instructions on when to seek urgent help: suicidal thoughts, severe agitation, and signs of serotonin syndrome (rare).

The agent provides lifestyle education: sleep hygiene; limiting caffeine/nicotine/alcohol; stress-management strategies (breathing exercises, mindfulness/CBT tools); and consistent physical activity as tolerated.
The agent encourages ongoing scleroderma management and instructs prompt reporting of new/worsening cardiopulmonary symptoms that can mimic anxiety.
The agent schedules appropriate follow-up (typically 2–4 weeks after initiation or dose change) and includes a plan for dose adjustment based on response and tolerability.

minimal_paths.description: Typical presentation of anxiety in systemic sclerosis with normal basic labs; initiate sertraline after required safety checks and provide education and follow-up.
Basic labs already available and normal; proceed to diagnosis, safety checks, sertraline initiation, and counseling without re-ordering labs.

Patient prefers non-pharmacologic management initially; agent provides education, lifestyle/CBT referral suggestions, and follow-up while still completing safety assessment and discussing sertraline as an option.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs contraindication assessment within ASK (e.g., MAOI use, bipolar history, QT risk, severe hepatic disease) and documents it; still must run explicit CHECK_ALLERGY and CHECK_INTERACTION before PRESCRIBE per benchmark tools.
If labs are already normal, agent skips ORDER_LAB and uses GET_RESULTS to reference existing results; must not claim normal labs without verifying results.
Agent chooses a slower titration plan for sertraline (e.g., start low and increase after 1–2 weeks) due to sensitivity/side-effect concern; education and safety-netting remain required.
